sections.paragraph:
fut. יֶאֱֹור Jer 1:17, with suff. יַאַזְרֵנִי Job 30:18, to bind around, to gird, also, to be girded, to gird oneself. Arab. أَزَرَ to be strong, robust, and perhaps, also, prop. to be girded, to gird. Conj. II. to gird. Conj. III. to strengthen, to aid. Cognate roots, all of which have the sense of to bind together, to gird, to surround, are, אָסַר, (אָצַל) אָצַר, עָצַר, עָטַר, עָזַר, חָצַר, חָרַר, גָּרַר. It is used-(a) of the garment with which any one is girded, with an acc. of pers. Job 30:18.-(b) with an acc. of the member girded, Job 38:3, חֲלָצֶיךָ אֱזָר־נָא “gird up thy loins;” Job 40:7 Jer 1:17.-(c) with an acc. of the girdle or garment with which any one is girded, and figuratively, 1Sa 2:4, אָֽזְרוּ הַיִל “they are girded with strength.”

Niphal part. נֶאְזָר girded, Psa 65:7.

Piel, to gird, with two acc., one of the person, the other of the girdle, Psa 18:33, 40 Psa 18:40, וַתְּאַזְּרֵנִי חַיִל לַמִּלְחָמָה “thou hast girded me with might for the war;” Psa 30:12, וַתְּאַוְּרֵנִי שִׂמְחָה “thou hast girded (i.e. surrounded) me with gladness;” Isa 50:11, מְאַזְּרֵי זִיקֹות “girded (i.e. armed) with burning darts.” As to the construction of verbs of this kind with two accus. compare Lehrg. § 219, 1.

Hithpael, to gird oneself (i.e. arm oneself), to prepare for battle, Isa 8:9 with an accus. Psa 93:1 (with strength).
